channel.title: flove making-of — docs questions
channel.description: New docs design questions from flove interviews.

item.title: The modal "See docs →" links point to Spanish ids that don't exist in docs — how to fix?
item.description:
• Map to real category sections — Point each app to its existing docs anchor (#metaphysics, #science, #biology, #language, #psycosocial, #economy, #ecosystem). Fixes all links, no docs edits. • Add per-app anchors in docs — Insert an id per app and deep-link; most precise, much more work for 30+ apps. • Point all to docs top — Open docs at the top; simplest, least useful.
★ decided: Map to real category sections · ◇ recommended: Map to real category sections

item.title: What theming/branding approach for docs?
item.description:
• Dark theme (manual toggle) — Default dark, user can switch to light. Standard approach, moderate code. • Auto-detect system theme — Follow OS dark/light setting. No toggle needed, less user control. • Both (toggle + auto) — Auto-detect with manual override. Most flexible, most code. • Always light — No theming — always light. Simplest, but mismatches flove's dark aesthetic. • Always dark — No theming — always dark. Matches flove, simplest, no toggle.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Always dark

item.title: How should docs search work?
item.description:
• Client-side full-text (lunr.js) — Self-contained search, works offline, adds ~50KB JS. Flove-first approach. • Algolia DocSearch — Fast, hosted, free for OSS. External service dependency. • Google site search — Easy to set up, external, requires internet. Simple but not flove-first. • None (just nav) — No search — rely on good navigation and cross-links. Simplest.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: None (just nav)

item.title: Should docs have cross-links between pages?
item.description:
• Inline links in text — Natural hyperlinks within content. Best UX, organic discovery. • Related-docs section — Explicit 'Related' block at the bottom of each page. Structured but rigid. • Both — Inline + related section. Maximum discoverability, maximum maintenance. • None — No cross-links — each page is standalone. Simplest.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Inline links in text

item.title: What navigation pattern for docs?
item.description:
• Sidebar — Persistent left sidebar with section links. Standard docs pattern, always visible. • Top nav bar — Horizontal nav at the top. Cleaner, less space, but limited depth. • Breadcrumbs — Path-based breadcrumbs at the top. Good for deep hierarchies, less for flat ones. • None needed — No dedicated nav — just links in content. Simplest.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Sidebar

item.title: Should docs include code examples?
item.description:
• Copy-paste snippets — Short, focused code blocks. Easy to scan, easy to copy. • Full file examples — Complete HTML files users can download. More complete, heavier. • None — No code examples — just prose. Simplest.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Copy-paste snippets

item.title: Should docs have a contribution guide?
item.description:
• Yes, contribution guide — Explain how to contribute — structure, style, PR process. Encourages community. • No, Marc-only — Keep docs editing to Marc only. Simpler, more control.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: No, Marc-only

item.title: How should users get help or contact support?
item.description:
• Email link — Simple contact form or mailto link. Direct, personal. • GitHub issues — Public issue tracker. Transparent, searchable, but requires GitHub account. • Both — Email for private, GitHub for public. Most flexible. • None — No explicit support channel — users find Marc through the community.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: None

item.title: How should docs be maintained over time?
item.description:
• Scheduled reviews — Regular audits (quarterly). Proactive, catches drift early. • On-demand only — Update when something breaks or changes. Reactive, less work. • Both — Scheduled audits + on-demand fixes. Most thorough.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: On-demand only

item.title: Should docs support extensibility (plugins)?
item.description:
• Plugin system — Document how to build plugins/modules. Useful for developers, adds scope. • Just standards — Document the standards only — no extensibility docs. Focused. • Both — Standards + extensibility. Comprehensive but more work.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Just standards

item.title: What is the primary audience for flove docs?
item.description:
• Developers — Technical docs — API references, code examples, architecture. Detailed, code-heavy. • Users — End-user docs — how to use flove apps. Practical, less technical. • Contributors — How to contribute to flove — standards, workflows, PR process. Community-focused. • All equally — No primary audience — serve everyone equally. Broad, less focused.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Developers

item.title: How should docs handle versioning?
item.description:
• Single 'latest' — One set of docs, always current. Simplest, no version sprawl. • Version per release — Snapshot docs per version. Accurate, but maintenance heavy. • Inline version markers — Tag sections with 'available since FloveX26-3'. Lightweight, informative.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Single 'latest'
